title: Credential Compromise via Frankensteined Identity Assembly from Multiple Breaches
narrative: Fraudsters combine identity fragments sourced from different data breaches or phishing campaigns to construct seemingly legitimate transactions. Each individual element (email, billing address, device attributes) passes standalone validation checks because each fragment is authentic and associated with real accounts. However, these elements have never appeared together in a genuine cardholder transaction. The card exhibits elevated aggregation counters indicating usage across multiple disparate merchant contexts while the assembled identity shows no coherent historical relationship. Device fingerprinting reveals the session lacks continuity with prior cardholder patterns. The transaction amount and velocity remain within acceptable bounds to avoid behavioral triggers, but engineered features detecting identity-cohesion anomalies and card-aggregation patterns expose the fraudulent assembly.
indicators: D1 = 0 indicating first-time pairing of device with this card
C1 > 60 (p90 threshold) showing elevated card-counter aggregation across rolling window
C13 > 24 (p90 threshold) indicating card used across many distinct merchant-product combinations
card1_distinct_addr1 >= 5 showing card associated with multiple billing addresses in recent window
V258 > 4.2 (Vesta engineered identity-cohesion score flagging mismatched element assembly)
addr1_email_domain_mismatch = 1 where billing address geography and email provider region never co-occurred historically
TransactionAmt between 50-200 (within p25-p75 range) masking fraud through normal amount behavior
indicator_explanations: The device connecting this transaction has never been seen with this card before
Card-level activity counter far exceeds typical cardholder baseline usage patterns
Card shows usage spanning an unusually diverse set of merchant and product categories
The card has been linked to multiple different billing addresses recently
Engineered feature detects identity elements that lack historical co-occurrence patterns
Billing address location and email domain geography have never appeared together for this cardholder
Transaction amount fits within the cardholder's normal spending range to avoid detection
summary: Each identity element validates individually but the combination has never existed together. Card aggregation counters reveal cross-merchant usage inconsistent with cardholder history while amounts remain unremarkable.
reasoning: Legitimate cardholders use consistent identity combinations across transactions. When valid fragments from disparate sources assemble for the first time alongside elevated card-counter patterns, it indicates credential compromise where the fraudster has stitched together stolen data from multiple breaches to bypass individual verification checks.
